title: Excess nitrogen from croplands
subtitle: "Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.
caption:
Excess nitrogen from croplands

"Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.

The choropleth map displays global excess nitrogen from croplands for the year 2009, using a color scale ranging from light yellow for 100,000 tonnes to dark brown for 25 million tonnes. China and India appear in the darkest brown band, indicating values at or near the 25 million tonne upper limit. The United States, Brazil, and parts of Western Europe, including France and Germany, fall within the orange bands corresponding to the 1 million to 10 million tonne range. Canada, Australia, and Russia are shaded in lighter orange or yellow tones, placing them generally between the 100,000 and 1 million tonne bands. Much of Sub-Saharan Africa and Central Asia is colored in the lightest yellow, signifying values near the 100,000 tonne threshold. Greenland and several countries in central Africa and the Middle East are hatched, denoting no data. The legend includes a distinct blue category for nitrogen mining, which is not visibly applied to any country on this specific map. The distribution shows a concentration of higher values in East and South Asia, with moderate values across North America and Europe, and lower values across most of Africa and Oceania.
